recipes.name: Jollof Rice
recipes.why_it_moves: It is colorful, smoky, easy to serve in a big pan, and naturally starts the friendly country-pride conversation.
recipes.caption: Whose jollof wins this table tonight?
recipes.hosting_move: Serve from one wide tray with grilled chicken, fried plantain, salad, and extra pepper sauce on the side.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot low across the rice so the red grains, browned protein, and plantain edges fill the frame.

recipes.name: Thieboudienne
recipes.why_it_moves: Whole fish, tomato rice, and vegetables make a communal platter that looks complete before anyone touches it.
recipes.caption: A Senegalese rice table that deserves the center spot.
recipes.hosting_move: Plate the fish whole over rice and arrange vegetables like a crown instead of scattering them.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot overhead so the fish, rice, cabbage, carrot, and cassava read as one composed platter.

recipes.name: Attiéké with Grilled Fish
recipes.why_it_moves: Fluffy attieke, grilled fish, onions, tomato, and hot piment create a plate with texture, color, and street-food energy.
recipes.caption: Attieke, fish, piment, done properly.
recipes.hosting_move: Keep attieke fluffy and warm, then finish with a bright onion-tomato salad and lemon wedges.
recipes.photo_angle: Frame the fish diagonally with attieke in the foreground and piment visible in a small bowl.

recipes.name: Kedjenou
recipes.why_it_moves: Kedjenou wins attention because the method is the story: no added water, tight seal, slow shake, deep sauce.
recipes.caption: No water, no rush, just the pot doing the work.
recipes.hosting_move: Open the pot at the table if you can; the steam and aroma are part of the drama.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot the opened pot with glossy sauce clinging to chicken and vegetables.

recipes.name: Kelewele
recipes.why_it_moves: Spiced fried plantain is quick, shareable, golden, and perfect for grazing while bigger dishes finish.
recipes.caption: One bowl of kelewele is never enough.
recipes.hosting_move: Serve hot in a lined bowl with roasted peanuts or toothpicks so people can keep reaching.
recipes.photo_angle: Use a close crop that catches caramelized corners and spice flecks.

recipes.name: Banku and Tilapia
recipes.why_it_moves: Charred tilapia, smooth banku, pepper sauce, and hand-eaten serving make it instantly social.
recipes.caption: Tilapia, banku, shito, and no overthinking.
recipes.hosting_move: Put shito and fresh pepper sauce in separate small bowls so guests can choose their heat.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot the fish whole with banku tucked beside it and sauce catching light.

recipes.name: Cachupa Rica
recipes.why_it_moves: Corn, beans, sausage, meat, and vegetables give this Cape Verdean classic a generous, cooked-all-day feel.
recipes.caption: Cachupa rica is the kind of bowl that tells on your patience.
recipes.hosting_move: Serve family-style, then fry leftovers the next morning if there is anything left.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot a deep spoonful lifting corn, beans, and meat from the pot.

recipes.name: Koshari
recipes.why_it_moves: Rice, pasta, lentils, chickpeas, tomato sauce, vinegar, and fried onions create a high-contrast bowl people want to mix.
recipes.caption: Egypt built the perfect carb stack.
recipes.hosting_move: Keep fried onions separate until serving so the crunch lands fresh.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot before mixing with onion crunch piled high and sauce streaking down one side.

recipes.name: Chicken Tagine with Preserved Lemons & Olives
recipes.why_it_moves: Preserved lemon, olives, herbs, and slow chicken make a fragrant centerpiece that looks elegant without fuss.
recipes.caption: Preserved lemon changes the whole room.
recipes.hosting_move: Bring the tagine or pot to the table closed, then uncover it in front of guests.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot from above just after uncovering, with lemon peel and olives clearly visible.

recipes.name: Couscous Royal
recipes.why_it_moves: Steamed couscous, vegetables, meats, and broth create the kind of abundant platter people gather around.
recipes.caption: Couscous royal means the table is not playing small.
recipes.hosting_move: Mound couscous first, then arrange meats and vegetables in visible sections before ladling broth.
recipes.photo_angle: Use a wide overhead shot so the abundance reads immediately.

recipes.name: Lablabi
recipes.why_it_moves: Bread, chickpeas, cumin broth, harissa, egg, tuna, olive oil, and lemon make a customizable bowl with street-food personality.
recipes.caption: Lablabi is comfort food with options.
recipes.hosting_move: Set toppings out separately and let people build heat, richness, and crunch their way.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot a half-built bowl with egg, harissa, and torn bread still visible.

recipes.name: Doro Wat
recipes.why_it_moves: Deep berbere sauce, slow onions, chicken, egg, and injera make it feel ceremonial before the first bite.
recipes.caption: Doro wat is not weeknight energy. It is occasion energy.
recipes.hosting_move: Serve with enough injera for scooping and place the egg where every plate can see it.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot close so the red sauce gloss, chicken, and egg become the whole story.

recipes.name: Injera
recipes.why_it_moves: Injera turns a meal into a shared surface, making every stew and vegetable feel connected.
recipes.caption: The plate is bread. The bread is the table.
recipes.hosting_move: Layer extra rolls beside the platter so guests never hesitate to scoop.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot the spiral texture or a platter where injera peeks under several stews.

recipes.name: Kitfo
recipes.why_it_moves: Kitfo stands out because it is direct, rich, and confident, with mitmita, niter kibbeh, and soft cheese or greens around it.
recipes.caption: Kitfo is for people who came ready.
recipes.hosting_move: Offer the doneness clearly and serve with ayib, gomen, and warm injera for balance.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot tight on the seasoned beef with ayib and greens framing the color.

recipes.name: Nyama Choma
recipes.why_it_moves: Roasted meat, kachumbari, ugali, salt, and hands-on eating make it one of the most social plates in East Africa.
recipes.caption: Nyama choma is not rushed. That is the point.
recipes.hosting_move: Slice the meat just before serving and keep kachumbari sharp enough to cut through the grill.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot a board of sliced meat with kachumbari tucked close and char visible.

recipes.name: Langouste a la Vanille
recipes.why_it_moves: Comorian lobster with vanilla is unexpected, luxurious, and memorable enough to become a conversation starter.
recipes.caption: Vanilla on lobster, because the islands said so.
recipes.hosting_move: Keep the sauce glossy and light so vanilla perfumes the seafood instead of burying it.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot the lobster shell-on with sauce pooled lightly and herbs for contrast.

recipes.name: Bunny Chow
recipes.why_it_moves: A hollowed loaf filled with curry is portable, messy, nostalgic, and made for hand-held sharing photos.
recipes.caption: Durban built the curry bowl you can eat.
recipes.hosting_move: Serve with the bread plug on top and sambal beside it so the reveal feels playful.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot the curry spilling slightly over the bread edge.

recipes.name: Bobotie
recipes.why_it_moves: Spiced mince, fruit sweetness, and custard topping make bobotie familiar and surprising at once.
recipes.caption: Sweet, spiced, golden, and very South African.
recipes.hosting_move: Let it rest before slicing so the custard top stays clean and camera-ready.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot one neat square with yellow rice, chutney, and the golden top visible.

recipes.name: Moambe Chicken
recipes.why_it_moves: Moambe chicken brings glossy palm-nut sauce, deep color, and a table-friendly richness that makes rice or fufu feel essential.
recipes.caption: That palm sauce is the whole conversation.
recipes.hosting_move: Serve sauce generously, then keep extra on the side because people will ask.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot chicken half-covered in sauce with a starch catching the edge of the plate.

recipes.name: Ndolé
recipes.why_it_moves: Ndole feels rich, layered, and celebratory, especially when shrimp, fish, meat, and plantain join the plate.
recipes.caption: Cameroon brought greens with authority.
recipes.hosting_move: Balance the bitterleaf and peanut richness with ripe plantain or miondo on the side.
recipes.photo_angle: Shoot close so the greens, peanuts, shrimp, and plantain contrast clearly.
